title: Static variables in module-level functions
body:
The **SystemVerilog** standard states that:

> **13.4.2 Static and automatic functions**
     Functions defined within a module, interface, program, or package default to being static, with all declared
items being statically allocated. These items shall be shared across all uses of the function executing
concurrently. __(see, for instance, [IEEE Std 1800-2012](http://www.ece.uah.edu/~gaede/cpe526/2012%20System%20Verilog%20Language%20Reference%20Manual.pdf))__

Therefore I expect that this module:
```
module mytest;
   function /* static */ integer mygenerator;
      /* static */ integer state = 5;
      mygenerator = ++state;
   endfunction
   initial
     for (integer n=0; n<=3; ++n)
       $display(mygenerator());
endmodule
```
would display the numbers from 1 to 4. 

Indeed, this is the case if I test it with **iverilog**:
```
>> iverilog -g2005-sv mytest.sv 
mytest.sv:3: warning: Static variable initialization requires explicit lifetime in this context.
>> ./a.out
         6
         7
         8
         9
```
But testing it with **verilator --prefix DUT -cc mytest.sv** and a minimal testbench in C++:
```
#include "DUT.h" // from verilator
int main() { DUT dut; dut.eval(); return 0; }
```
displays `6` four times to the screen, as if variable `state` inside function `mygenerator` was automatic and reinitialised to .

I haven't found reference to **static variables** either in verilator's manual or in the FAQs. Is this a known limitation? Can it be lifted? Rationale: having access to static functions (with static local variables) allows to parametrically build circuits in a SystemVerilog's generate block when components' interconnections are algorithmically computable, but for which no closed formula exists.

_What 'verilator --version' are you using?_

I tested with a locally installed `Verilator 5.003 devel rev v5.002-83-g66d85b338

_What OS and distribution are you using?_

Linux Ubuntu Kinetic Kudu = Ubuntu 22.10
